trust_assumption: A relying party that has been provisioned a hybrid post-quantum certificate or hybrid key-exchange group (classical+ML-KEM/ML-DSA) trusts that because the peer is PQC-capable, the negotiated session inherited the quantum-resistant half of the hybrid, so it records the connection as 'PQC-protected' in its posture and compliance state even when only the classical component actually established the shared secret.
violation: An on-path attacker strips or fails the PQC half of the hybrid (drops the ML-KEM keyshare, forces a classical-only group, or presents the classical-only fallback chain a dual-stack deployment still accepts for legacy reach), and the relying party silently completes a purely classical session while its telemetry, audit log, and crypto-agility dashboard still report a quantum-safe handshake — leaving a harvestable session mislabeled as protected.

techniques.name: Hybrid keyshare strip forcing classical-only PQC fallback
PQC posture mislabeling of a downgraded hybrid session

rationale: The 2025-2027 migration wave deploys hybrid certs and hybrid key exchange in dual-stack mode precisely so that PQC-incapable peers still connect, which means a classical-only outcome is a designed-in success, not an error. The dangerous seam is not the downgrade itself but that the relying party's posture/compliance accounting credits the connection as quantum-safe based on the cert's hybrid *capability* rather than the session's actual negotiated *outcome*, so an attacker who forces the classical path also forges the organization's record of being PQC-protected. This is distinct from generic TLS downgrade because the violated trust is the PQC-readiness bookkeeping layer that migration programs rely on to declare risk retired; no scanner today reconciles 'hybrid cert present' against 'PQC component actually used per session', and no IDS flags a hybrid-capable peer that settled on classical.
suggested_research: Build a session-level reconciler that parses negotiated KEM groups and signature algorithms from live handshakes and diffs them against the hybrid capability advertised in the served certificate, alerting when a PQC-capable endpoint completes classical-only; red-team a dual-stack hybrid deployment by selectively dropping ML-KEM keyshares and confirming the compliance dashboard still reports quantum-safe.

trust_assumption: A crypto-agility control plane (centralized cipher-policy service, KMS algorithm profile, or fleet crypto-policy CRD) is trusted by every workload that reads it to only ratchet algorithm strength forward, so workloads cache and apply the distributed policy without re-validating that a newly fetched 'allowed algorithms' set is not weaker than the one they were already enforcing.
violation: An attacker with write access to the agility policy store (or who replays an older signed policy version) pushes a policy revision that re-enables a deprecated suite or lowers the minimum negotiated group, and the fleet's workloads apply it as an authoritative forward update because the agility mechanism designed to make algorithms changeable has no monotonic floor — making the very lever built to strengthen crypto into a one-command fleet-wide weakening primitive.

techniques.name: Crypto-agility policy rollback to deprecated suite
Signed-policy replay reintroducing weak algorithm floor

rationale: Crypto-agility is the headline answer to PQC migration: orgs are standing up central policy planes in 2025-2027 so they can swap algorithms without redeploying code. But agility is bidirectional by construction, and the deployment model is 'workloads trust the latest policy the plane hands them.' Nobody is treating the algorithm-strength dimension as a monotonic counter the way anti-rollback treats firmware versions, so a compromised or replayed policy revision quietly relaxes the entire fleet at once and looks like a normal config push. This is a new seam distinct from per-connection downgrade and from the excluded crypto-agility-rollback hint's framing of negotiation: here the trust violated is the distribution channel's implicit monotonicity, landing on every workload that treats centralized crypto policy as authoritative. No policy plane enforces a strength floor or signs a non-decreasing version, and SIEMs do not parse policy diffs for strength regressions.
suggested_research: Prototype a monotonic-strength guard that ranks each pushed crypto policy on a partial order of algorithm strength and refuses or quarantines any revision below the highest previously-applied floor, with attestation that the floor counter is tamper-evident; demonstrate fleet weakening via a replayed older signed policy against a KMS-driven cipher profile.

trust_assumption: An organization that enables TLS session-key export (SSLKEYLOGFILE-style decryption for observability, WAF, DLP, or eBPF middleboxes) trusts that the exported per-session keys are ephemeral operational artifacts that live only as long as the flow inspection needs them, so it pipes them into the same log/observability and long-term storage pipeline as ordinary telemetry without a separate confidentiality lifecycle.
violation: The exported session keys are aggregated into centralized observability/SIEM and cold archival storage alongside passively-captured ciphertext flows, so an attacker (or a future quantum adversary) who reaches the archive obtains both the long-lived stored ciphertext and the very keys to decrypt it — collapsing the harvest-now-decrypt-later defense organizations think PQC migration gives them, because the plaintext-equivalent keys were retained next to the data the whole time.

techniques.name: Exported TLS session keys co-archived with captured ciphertext
Observability keylog pipeline as long-lived decryption oracle

rationale: Enterprises increasingly export TLS keys to inspect their own encrypted traffic (eBPF observability, cloud-native WAFs, DLP), and separately are migrating to PQC to defend against harvest-now-decrypt-later. The overlooked seam is that the keylog export pipeline and the packet/flow capture pipeline both feed the same SIEM and the same multi-year cold storage, so an org that believes PQC has retired its HNDL exposure has actually been co-locating plaintext-equivalent keys with the ciphertext in long-lived storage — no quantum computer required, just archive access. This is distinct from the passive-capture HNDL hint because the violated trust is the assumption that exported keys are ephemeral; the danger is the archival co-location bridge, which no DLP/observability product treats as a confidentiality boundary and no retention policy isolates. There is no tooling that detects session keys and ciphertext sharing a retention tier and no detection for keylog material leaking into long-term stores.
suggested_research: Map real observability/WAF stacks for where SSLKEYLOG-equivalent material lands and whether it shares a retention class with captured flows; build a storage-classifier that flags any tier holding both decryption keys and corresponding ciphertext, and propose an ephemeral key-escrow with enforced short TTL distinct from the telemetry lake.

trust_assumption: An edge-compute platform (V8-isolate Workers, functions-at-edge) trusts that the per-request isolate it hands a tenant's code starts from a clean, request-scoped global state, so tenant code freely caches secrets, decrypted tokens, and per-user context in module-level globals on the assumption that reuse only ever serves the same tenant's subsequent requests on the same warmed isolate.
violation: The platform's isolate-reuse / cross-request global persistence (warm isolates kept alive across requests, or a module-scope variable surviving between invocations) is observed by a co-resident or follow-on request from a different tenant or user routed to the same warmed isolate, so module-level secrets and decrypted context from request N leak into the execution context of request N+1 that the tenant never intended to share — a serverless analog of cold-start bleed but at the isolate-global granularity edge platforms uniquely expose.

techniques.name: Edge isolate module-global secret bleed across requests

rationale: Edge platforms aggressively reuse warm V8 isolates and reused execution contexts to kill cold-start latency, and their developer guidance does warn against global state — but in 2025-2027 the explosion of edge code that decrypts tokens, holds KV-fetched secrets, and caches per-user auth context in module globals turns that performance optimization into a confidentiality seam. The trust violated is context-scoping: developers and the platform implicitly assume request isolation, while the isolate model actually grants request N+1 inherited access to whatever request N left in scope. This is a distinct gap from serverless cold-start bleed framed for VM/container functions because edge isolates share a single process and reuse globals at far finer granularity and far higher reuse rates, with no per-request scrubbing. There is no tooling that detects module-global secret retention across edge invocations and no platform-side or detection signal for cross-request context inheritance.
suggested_research: Build a static/dynamic analyzer for edge Worker code that flags secrets or decrypted context persisting in module scope across the request boundary, plus a runtime probe that measures whether warmed isolates leak prior-request globals to a differently-keyed follow-on request; quantify reuse rates on a major edge platform.

trust_assumption: A CDN/edge cache trusts that its computed cache key (the normalized subset of request attributes it hashes) fully captures every input the origin used to authorize and personalize a response, so a cache hit for a given key is safe to serve to any subsequent requester that produces the same key, regardless of headers, tenancy claims, or edge-function transforms not folded into the key.
violation: An attacker crafts requests where an authorization- or tenant-determining input (an edge-function-injected header, a tenant token, a Vary-omitted field) influences the personalized/authorized origin response but is excluded from the cache key, so the edge caches a victim-scoped or differently-tenanted response and serves it cross-tenant to attacker requests — a confused-deputy disclosure born from the mismatch between what authorizes the response and what keys the cache.

techniques.name: Edge-function-injected header excluded from cache key

rationale: Modern edge stacks let functions-at-edge mutate requests (inject identity headers, rewrite tenancy) and personalize responses before and after the cache, while the cache key is a separately-configured normalized hash. In 2025-2027 deployments the set of inputs that authorize a response has drifted away from the set folded into the cache key, especially when edge middleware adds tenant context the cache config never learned about. The violated trust is the cache's assumption that its key is a complete authorization fingerprint; the result is a confused deputy that serves one tenant's authorized content to another. This is broader and more current than classic web-cache-deception/poisoning because the divergence is now manufactured by programmable edge functions rather than just header quirks. Cache-key auditing tooling is only nascent and there is no detection that correlates an authorization-relevant input the origin honored against the keying config the edge actually used.
suggested_research: Develop a differential probe that enumerates request attributes, classifies which ones change the authorized/personalized origin response, and diffs that set against the live cache-key and Vary configuration to surface unkeyed authorization inputs; demonstrate cross-tenant cache serve through an edge-function-injected tenant header excluded from the key.

trust_assumption: A workload that pulls short-lived credentials from a secrets manager (dynamic DB creds, leased cloud tokens) trusts that a credential it successfully cached is valid and authoritative for its full advertised TTL, and the issuing manager trusts that revoking/rotating the old secret at rotation time atomically retires it everywhere — so neither side accounts for the window in which a rotated-out credential is still accepted by a downstream resource that has not yet refreshed its trust material.
violation: An attacker who captured a leased credential continues to use it during the rotation overlap window, because the secrets manager's rotation marks a new secret active but the downstream datastore/service still honors the prior one until its own cache or grace period expires, and the client meanwhile keeps presenting a cached lease past its real revocation — turning the rotation seam into a live re-use window where a credential is simultaneously 'rotated out' at the manager and 'still good' at the resource.

techniques.name: Rotated-out lease accepted during downstream grace overlap
Client cache honoring credential past real revocation

rationale: Dynamic secrets and short-TTL leases are sold as the answer to credential theft, but the operational reality in 2025-2027 is that rotation is never atomic across the three parties: the manager (Vault/cloud secrets), the client cache, and the downstream resource that validates the credential each maintain independent notions of validity, and rotation deliberately overlaps old and new to avoid outages. The violated trust is the assumption that 'rotated' means 'no longer accepted'; the overlap window is a designed-in re-use opportunity that a captured credential exploits with no anomaly because both old and new are momentarily legitimate. This is distinct from generic credential theft and from the excluded workload-identity-federation gap because the seam is the temporal non-atomicity of revocation across a distributed trust set, not the federation binding. Detection is only nascent (some managers log lease use) and no tooling reconciles 'manager says rotated' against 'resource still accepts' to expose the live overlap.
suggested_research: Instrument a Vault-or-cloud dynamic-secret pipeline to measure the real revocation-overlap window across manager, client cache, and downstream resource, and build a detector that flags use of a lease the manager already rotated but the resource still honors; prototype a rotation protocol that proves downstream retirement before marking a secret rotated.

trust_assumption: An eUICC/eSIM consumer device trusts that an operational profile delivered through the GSMA remote-SIM-provisioning (RSP) flow — once the activation code / QR / carrier-app handoff completes — was authorized by the legitimate subscriber for the legitimate operator, so the modem installs the profile and binds the device's network identity to it without independently re-verifying who actually initiated the provisioning.
violation: An attacker who hijacks the provisioning initiation (a phished activation code, a malicious carrier-app deep link, or an SM-DP+ address swap) drives the RSP flow to install an attacker-chosen profile or download a profile onto an attacker-controlled eUICC, so the subscriber's mobile network identity is silently bound to a profile the genuine subscriber never authorized — an eSIM-swap that needs no physical SIM and leaves the provenance of the provisioning request unverified at the device.

techniques.name: RSP activation-code/QR hijack installing unauthorized profile
SM-DP+ address swap redirecting profile download

rationale: eSIM is becoming default on flagship phones and IoT in 2025-2027 (carrier-only US iPhones, eSIM-only IoT modules), and the RSP trust chain authenticates the SM-DP+ server and the eUICC chip cryptographically but does not bind the provisioning *initiation* to verified subscriber intent — the activation code or carrier-app handoff is the soft seam. The violated trust is provenance of the request: the device treats a completed RSP handshake as proof the right human asked for this profile, when an attacker who captures the initiation can swap identity without ever touching hardware, defeating the physical-possession assumption that made classic SIM-swap need an insider. This is a fresh, lightly-tooled telecom seam distinct from generic SIM-swap because it lives in the remote-provisioning protocol layer and the device-side initiation binding, where no consumer detection exists and no tooling verifies initiation provenance.
suggested_research: Analyze the consumer RSP initiation path (activation codes, carrier-app deep links, default SM-DP+ resolution) for points where request provenance is unverified, and prototype a subscriber-bound initiation token plus a device-side prompt that surfaces the requesting operator identity and a fraud signal for out-of-band profile downloads; demonstrate profile redirection via a swapped SM-DP+ address.

trust_assumption: A 5G subscriber trusts that SUCI concealment (encrypting the SUPI/IMSI with the home network's public key before it ever leaves the device) makes their permanent identity unlinkable on the radio interface, so the privacy guarantee of the standard is treated as protecting against the IMSI-catcher tracking that plagued earlier generations.
violation: An attacker correlates the side-channel and protocol residue that SUCI does not conceal — stable per-device parameters, fixed home-network routing identifiers, deterministic SUCI re-computation patterns under a misconfigured null-scheme or reused randomness, and timing/capability fingerprints — to re-link successive concealed identifiers back to one subscriber across sessions, defeating the unlinkability the SUCI design is trusted to provide without ever recovering the cleartext SUPI.

techniques.name: SUCI cross-session linkability via unconcealed routing/capability residue

rationale: 5G SUCI is marketed as the cure for IMSI-catchers, and standalone 5G deployment ramps through 2025-2027, so operators and regulators increasingly treat the radio-layer identity-privacy box as checked. The seam is that SUCI conceals the SUPI but leaves a constellation of unconcealed or weakly-randomized fields (routing indicator, home-network PKI key id, device capability fingerprints, and — where operators ship the null scheme or weak randomness — recomputable concealed values) that an analytic adversary fuses to re-establish linkability across sessions. The violated trust is the unlinkability provenance of the concealed identifier itself. This is a current, untooled privacy gap distinct from classic IMSI-catching because the attack is correlation against the metadata the privacy scheme does not cover, not interception of cleartext. No tooling measures real-world SUCI linkability and no detection flags re-identification campaigns.
suggested_research: Capture SUCI exchanges across a controlled 5G SA testbed and measure cross-session linkability from routing indicators, capability fingerprints, and randomness quality, including operator configurations that fall back to the null scheme; build a linkability-scoring tool operators can run to validate that their SUCI deployment actually delivers unlinkability.

trust_assumption: A verifier of a keyless-signed OCI artifact (Sigstore/Fulcio short-lived cert tying a signature to an OIDC identity) trusts that the OIDC identity baked into the signing certificate corresponds to a legitimately-controlled, human- or pipeline-owned account, so an artifact whose transparency-log entry maps to a recognizable identity string is accepted as authentically published by that party.
violation: An attacker who controls a phishable, typosquatted, dormant, or workflow-impersonating OIDC identity (a compromised CI workflow ref, a look-alike email/GitHub identity, a reused org account) obtains a valid Fulcio certificate and signs a malicious artifact, and the verifier's policy admits it because the identity *string* matches an allowed pattern even though the binding from that OIDC identity to a trustworthy real-world publisher was never itself validated — the keyless model moved the trust to an OIDC identity that the verifier never authenticated the provenance of.

techniques.name: Phishable/typosquatted OIDC identity yielding valid keyless signature
Workflow-ref impersonation in keyless cert identity claim

rationale: Keyless signing (Sigstore/Fulcio/Rekor) is the fast-spreading 2025-2027 answer to 'no more long-lived signing keys,' and verifiers increasingly gate artifact admission on the OIDC identity embedded in the short-lived cert. The overlooked seam is that the security now rests entirely on the trustworthiness of that OIDC identity binding, which was itself phishable, typosquattable, or impersonable at the CI-workflow level — the verifier authenticates that *a* valid OIDC identity signed, not that the identity legitimately represents the publisher it claims. This is explicitly distinct from CA mis-issuance (no rogue CA needed; Fulcio behaves correctly) and from SLSA provenance scope: the violated trust is the identity-binding semantics of keyless signatures and the verifier's pattern-matching policy. Identity-allowlist tooling is only nascent and brittle to look-alikes, and there is no detection for keyless signatures from newly-seen, dormant, or impersonating identities.
suggested_research: Build an identity-binding risk scorer for keyless-signature policies that flags look-alike, newly-registered, dormant, or workflow-ref-mismatched OIDC identities and requires reputation/age evidence before admission; demonstrate admitting a malicious artifact via a typosquatted OIDC identity that satisfies a naive identity-pattern verification policy.
